rubric: Strict kid engagement: surprise, concrete imagery, no I'm/My/Santa, no textbook tone.

animals.en: Aardvarks slurp ants with a sticky tongue longer than a ruler, turning anthills into all-you-can-eat buffets.
Abyssal spiderfish sprawl on the seafloor with long spiny fins, looking like spiders that traded webs for deep-sea chill.
Acorn worms look like slim acorns that spit sticky mucus nets to catch food—gross, but brilliant for a worm.
Adélie penguins steal pebbles from neighbors' nests—naughty rock collectors!
Albatrosses glide for hours without flapping—ocean wanderers with airplane wings!
Alpaca fleece is softer than sheep wool and comes in 22 natural colors—nature's rainbow yarn!
Green anacondas are the heaviest snakes—they swim like silent river logs!
Angelfish glide through coral like flat golden frisbees, turning corners with fins that sparkle in sunbeams.
Anglerfish dangle glowing lures to attract prey in the dark deep sea!
Giant anteaters have 2-ft tongues that flick in and out 150 times a minute!
Arctic foxes turn snow-white in winter and brown in summer—seasonal outfit swap!
Arctic hares sprint up to 40 mph in snow—white lightning with long legs!
An Arctic wolf can vanish in blowing snow with its white coat, moving so quietly that the tundra feels like it’s holding its breath.
Armadillos jump straight up when startled—surprise bounce armor!
Aye-ayes tap trees with a long middle finger to find grubs—woodpecker fingers!
Badgers dig tunnel homes with bedrooms and pantries—underground mansions!
Barn owls screech instead of hoot and hear mice under snow!
Barnacles glue their heads to rocks and kick food into their mouths with feathery legs—upside-down filter fans.
Barracudas flash silver and zip through reefs like underwater rockets!
Barreleye fish have see-through heads so you can peek at their green tube eyes—like a fish with a glass helmet.
Basket stars open branching arms like living lace nets, catching plankton snacks from passing currents.
Beaked whales can dive deeper than almost any mammal on one breath—like holding your nose for a roller-coaster ride to the abyss.
Beavers build dams that create ponds—animal engineers changing whole valleys!
Belugas are white 'sea canaries' that chirp, whistle, and mimic sounds!
Bengal tiger stripes are as unique as fingerprints, and a roar can rumble through the air like a giant drumbeat.
Big red jellies drift like giant crimson umbrellas in the deep, pulsing slow and spooky through the dark.
Black bears climb trees like giant teddy bears looking for honey!
Black swallowers eat fish twice their size—their stomach stretches like a balloon!
Blobfish look like grumpy jelly on land but normal in deep water pressure!
Blue jays mimic hawk calls to scare other birds away from food!
Blue tangs zip through reefs in electric blue with sunny tail tips, like swimming highlighters with fins.
Boas squeeze prey gently with muscles, then swallow whole—no chewing needed!
Bobcats have short bobbed tails and tufted ears, sneaking through forests like mini lynxes on tiptoe.
Border collies are brainy herders that use a laser-stare to guide sheep—like furry air-traffic controllers on the farm.
Dolphins call each other by signature whistles—each has a unique name!
Bowheads may live over 200 years—the oldest mammals on Earth!
Boxfish swim in tiny armored boxes with puckered lips, wiggling through reefs like little underwater robots.
Brain corals grow wrinkly mounds that look like giant thinking caps, building reef cities one bump at a time.
Brisingid starfish lift many arms into the current like a feather duster, catching drifting snacks in the deep dark.
Brown bears catch salmon leaping up waterfalls—nature's ultimate fishing show!
Bush babies leap 6 ft between branches with huge eyes like night goggles!
Butterflyfish swim in bright pairs through coral, flashing stripes like underwater butterflies on a date.
The cacomistle tiptoes on branches at night, using its ringed tail like a balance rope in a fuzzy circus act.
Capuchins use rocks to crack nuts—little monkeys with stone hammers!
Male cardinals wear bright red feathers like feathered superheroes at feeders!
Caribou herds migrate farther than any land mammal—thousands of miles a year!
Chameleons shoot tongues faster than you blink to catch bugs!
Chickens can remember over 100 faces—including yours—and they talk to each other with more than 30 different clucks!
Chimaeras are “ghost sharks” from deep water, with smooth bodies and strange snouts that make them look like ancient ocean secrets.
Chimps use sticks as tools to fish termites—wild scientists with snacks!
Chipmunks stuff cheeks with nuts until they look like furry footballs!
Civets smell like popcorn from scent glands—stinky but surprisingly snacky!
Cleaner shrimp run fish spas, picking off itchy parasites while customers hold still for a free tickle clean.
Clownfish live safely in stinging anemones—best friends with a squishy fortress!
Cockatiels sport crest mohawks that rise when surprised or happy!
Coffinfish inflate like spiky balloons and walk on fin-feet on the seafloor!
Colossal squids have swiveling hooks on tentacles—the kraken is real!
Comb jellies rainbow-pulse rows of cilia like living fiber-optic ribbons!
Conchs have a shiny pink interior and a long eye stalk that peeks around!
Cookiecutter sharks bite round plugs out of whales, submarines, and even sonar!
Cows have four stomachs, so they can turn grass into energy like a super-powered salad factory!
Coyotes yip and howl from forests to city parks, proving you can be wild, clever, and still find dinner anywhere.
Cusk eels hide in muddy burrows like ribbon ninjas, peeking out with wiggly heads to ambush passing snacks.
Cuttlefish ripple colors across their skin like living TV screens!
Deep-sea amphipods bounce along the seafloor like alien popcorn, munching whatever tasty crumbs drift down from above.
Some deep sea cucumbers glow when touched—living glow sticks of the abyss!
Deep sea jellies drift like glowing ghosts with tentacles longer than buses!
Donkeys have huge ears that work like satellite dishes—they hear danger from far away!
Dragonfish flash red and blue lights invisible to most prey—secret night vision battle!
Ducks have waterproof feathers—special oil from a gland near the tail keeps them dry underwater!
Dumbo octopuses flap ear fins like Disney elephants in the abyss!
Eagles spot tiny prey from skyscraper heights, then dive with talons that grip harder than your strongest handshake.
An elephant’s trunk has thousands of muscles, so it can pick up a peanut, spray a shower, or give a gentle high-five.
Male elephant seals roar and jiggle noses in epic beach battles!
Male elk bugle in fall—a wild trumpet call that echoes through mountains!
Emperor dads balance eggs on feet for two months in Antarctic blizzards!
Ermine wear a snowy white coat in winter with a black-tipped tail like a paintbrush!
The faceless cusk seems to have no face because its mouth hides underneath, like a deep-sea trapdoor full of surprises.
Peregrine falcons dive faster than race cars—over 200 mph!
Fangtooth fish pack the biggest teeth for their size in the ocean—a grin that could win a scary contest.
Fennec foxes have giant ears that radiate heat and hear beetles under sand!
Ferrets do a happy 'war dance'—bouncing and bumping when they're super excited!
Male fiddler crabs wave one giant claw like a violin bow to impress friends!
Fireflies blink bioluminescent codes to find friends on summer nights!
Flashlight fish have glowing spots under their eyes—they blink lights to talk!
Flounders begin life with eyes on both sides, then one eye slides over for the ultimate fish makeover.
Frilled sharks look like sea serpents with frilly gill slits!
Fruit bats spread seeds and pollen as they fly, helping new trees grow—like night gardeners with wings.
Geckos stick to ceilings with toe pads so sticky they can hold your weight scaled down!
A genet is a night-time tree acrobat, sliding and leaping with a long striped tail that looks like a sneaky balance pole.
Ghost sharks glide through deep reefs with spooky eyes and plate teeth, looking like ancient fish from a midnight story.
Giant clams shimmer with algae gardens inside their shells—living rainbows!
Giant squids battle sperm whales in the deep, turning the dark ocean into a real-life legend book.
Giant tube worms live at boiling deep vents with no gut—bacteria feed them!
Glass sponges build lacy skeletons from silica that look like spun-glass sculptures—fancy houses for deep water.
Goats are amazing climbers—they can scale steep cliffs and even balance on tiny ledges!
Goblin sharks shoot jaws forward like alien monsters—living fossils!
Golden retrievers fetch like champions and greet people with whole-body wags, as if happiness has a tail and fluffy ears.
Goldfish remember feeding tricks for months, busting the myth that they forget everything in three seconds flat.
Geese fly in a V formation so friends up front block wind for friends behind—teamwork in the sky!
Gorillas beat their chests to show strength—but they also giggle when tickled!
Great horned owls have ear tufts like horns and can carry prey heavier than themselves!
Great whites breach like missiles launching at seals!
Green iguanas can drop their tails when grabbed—it wiggles while they escape!
Grenadier fish cruise the abyss with giant heads and rat-long tails, looking like a wiggly ribbon that forgot to stop growing.
Guinea pigs pop and whistle when excited—they're tiny chat machines with fur!
Gulper eels swallow prey bigger than themselves—living parachute mouths!
Hammerhead sharks have T-shaped heads that work like stereo sonar!
Hamsters stuff food into cheek pouches that stretch all the way to their shoulders—nature's grocery bags!
Harbor seals banana-pose on rocks to keep flippers dry and warm!
Harp seal pups have fluffy white coats for two weeks—snowball babies on ice!
Hatchetfish are shaped like silver hatchets with glowing bellies, flashing lights to chat in the midnight sea.
Hawks see eight times sharper than humans—they spot a mouse from a skyscraper height!
When scared, hedgehogs roll into a spiky ball—like a living pin cushion!
Hermit crabs swap shells as they grow—mobile homes on the beach!
Horses can sleep standing up thanks to a special lock in their legs—like built-in kickstands!
Horseshoe crabs are older than dinosaurs, and their blue blood helps doctors check if medicines and tools are safe.
Howler monkeys have the loudest call of any land animal—it can be heard 3 miles away!
Hummingbirds hover like helicopters and their wings buzz at 80 beats per second!
Hyena giggles sound spooky but mean they're excited during a meal!
Jaguars have rosette spots and bite so strong they crack turtle shells!
Japanese spider crabs stretch legs wider than a car, earning the title of world’s widest crustacean superstar.
Jellyfish drift without bones or brains, pulsing like living lava lamps that sting if you boop the wrong tentacle.
Jerboas bounce on kangaroo legs and barely use front paws—desert pogo sticks!
Kinkajous hang upside down to eat honey—they're rainforest honey bears!
Koalas hug eucalyptus trees and nap up to 20 hours, saving energy like fluffy little phone chargers that sleep most of the day.
Krill gather in giant pink swarms that feed whales, fish, and penguins—tiny shrimps with a huge job in the food chain.
Antarctic krill swarm in pink clouds so huge that satellites can spot them from space—ocean glitter with legs.
Lanternfish sparkle with belly lights in the deep sea, and there are so many they might be the most common fish on Earth.
Leafcutter ants march carrying leaf pieces like tiny green sails!
Lemmings don't really jump off cliffs—they boom and bust in population cycles!
Ring-tailed lemurs sunbathe sitting yoga-style with arms out—warming up together!
Leopards drag prey up trees—dinner with a view away from lions!
Leopard seals have spotted coats and grinning faces, ruling icy waters as top hunters with a splash of drama.
A lion's roar can be heard 5 miles away—it shakes the savannah!
Lionfish fan venomous spines like underwater lions with fancy manes!
Lizardfish bury in sand with only eyes showing, then SNAP—one fast bite turns a passing fish into lunch.
Llamas hum when they’re happy, a soft song that sounds like a fuzzy friend saying ‘life is good today.’
Lobsters taste with their legs and can live longer than a century—clawed grandmas of the seafloor.
Lynx have snowshoe paws and black ear tufts like built-in antennae!
Macaws flash rainbow feathers and mate for life—flying jewels!
Manatees are gentle giants that munch seagrass for hours and sometimes give friends a slow-motion hug with their flippers.
Mandarinfish shimmer with psychedelic colors—tiny dragons of the reef!
Manta rays glide like flying carpets and leap entirely out of the water!
Mariana snailfish live deeper than any other fish—almost 27,000 feet down, where the pressure could squish a truck.
Moles are champion diggers—they can tunnel 18 ft in one hour underground!
The monophore snail vanished from science for decades, then popped up alive again—like the deep sea’s surprise comeback star!
Moorish idols trail a long ribbon fin behind them, swimming like dressed-up reef royalty in a fancy underwater parade.
Moose antlers can span 6 ft—wider than most cars!
Moray eels open and close their mouths to breathe—it looks scary, but it’s just underwater gulping, not a mean face.
Some moths wear disguise wings that copy wasps or owl eyes, throwing a costume party to trick hungry hunters.
Mice whiskers feel the world like radar, helping them zip through dark walls without bumping into trouble.
A mule is a horse-donkey mix with super steady feet, built to climb rocky trails like a mountain helper on four hooves.
Musk oxen circle babies in a shield wall when wolves attack—living fortress!
Mussels make super-sticky threads to glue themselves to rocks, holding on through waves like underwater Velcro.
Napoleon wrasse are reef giants with bumpy foreheads, and divers say some even come close like curious underwater puppies.
Narwhals grow unicorn tusks up to 10 ft—the tusk is actually a spiral tooth!
Nautiluses are living fossils with beautiful spiral shells and 90 tentacles!
Sea slugs wear rainbow colors and some steal stinging cells from jellyfish!
Oarfish are ribbon-thin sea serpents that wash ashore and start sea monster legends!
Octopuses have three hearts and blue blood—and solve puzzles to open jars!
Okapis have zebra stripes on legs but are giraffe relatives—forest giraffes in disguise!
Opossums play dead so convincingly that predators walk away—ultimate pretend nap!
Orangutans build fresh leaf nests to sleep in, like forest architects making a new bed in the treetops each night.
Orcas wear black-and-white tuxedo coats and hunt in tight family pods, chatting with clicks that sound like underwater Morse code.
Oysters can switch from female to male (and sometimes back), a real ocean trick that helps their shell-family make more babies.
Pangolins roll into scaly balls—real-life dragon eggs with legs!
Parakeets chatter in whistles and beeps, copying sounds like tiny feathered DJs remixing the room all day.
Parrots copy sounds, crack puzzles, and laugh like feathered comedians who never forget a favorite word.
Parrotfish crunch coral with beak teeth and poop sand—beach builders!
Pelicans scoop fish in huge bill pouches that hold 3 gallons of water!
Penguins can’t fly in air, but they rocket through water like torpedoes, steering with stiff wings and happy feet.
Pigs are super smart—some studies say they learn tricks as fast as dogs and even enjoy playtime!
Ping-pong tree sponges stack round balls like deep-sea Jenga, filtering the water all day to catch tiny snacks.
Ocean plankton may be tiny, but it helps make about half the oxygen you breathe—floating mini air-factories everywhere!
Tiny poison dart frogs wear neon colors that shout 'Don't eat me!'
Polar bears have black skin under white fur—sun heats them like solar panels!
Ponies may be small, but they are tough workers, pulling carts and carrying riders with strong legs that never quit.
Porcupines have 30,000 quills—each one a tiny spiky umbrella for protection!
Pot-bellied pigs love belly rubs and can learn names and tricks fast, proving that “farm cute” can also be super smart.
Ptarmigan swap to snow-white feathers in winter and grow extra feathery snowshoes on their feet for fluffy tundra walks.
Pufferfish puff up like spiky balloons when scared—don't-pop ocean balls!
Puffins carry dozens of fish crosswise in colorful beaks—clown birds of the sea!
Pythons have stretchy jaws that open wide enough to swallow deer!
Rabbits cool off through their big ears and can boing three feet high—like a furry pogo stick with a cotton tail.
Raccoons wash food in water—even when there's no water, they still 'wash'!
Rats giggle when tickled—scientists hear it with special microphones!
When scared they flash giant red eyes and orange feet—surprise superhero reveal!
Foxes use Earth's magnetic field to aim pounces through snow at hidden mice!
Reef sharks zip through coral alleys like quiet lifeguards, keeping the reef healthy by chasing sick fish away.
Reindeer hooves spread like snowshoes, helping them walk on soft snow, and their noses warm icy air before each breath.
River otters slide on snow and mud for fun—nature's playground slides!
Rockhoppers hop up cliffs with spiky yellow crests like punk rock feathers!
A rooster’s sunrise crow can travel far, like a feathered alarm clock announcing morning to the whole neighborhood.
Salamanders can regrow legs and tails—superhero amphibians!
Sand dollars are flat urchins with tiny hairs that move them slowly, like living coins crawling across the seafloor.
Scallops swim by clapping their shells together—jet-powered shellfish!
Scorpions glow green under UV light—secret disco armor!
Sea anemones look like flowers but sting like traps—pretty and powerful!
Sea angels flutter wing-like fins—they look like glowing fairies in icy water!
Sea fans wave in the current like lace curtains, catching tiny snacks while doing an underwater ballet.
Sea lions bark and clap their flippers on rocks like aquatic performers!
Sea otters float on backs cracking shellfish with rocks—cute tool users!
Sea pens stand on the seafloor like old feather quills, swaying together when the current says ‘dance.’
Sea pigs waddle on tube feet across the abyss, looking like pink potatoes that grew legs and joined a marching band.
Sea sponges have no brain or bones, yet they pump and filter tons of water, slurping tiny food like living kitchen sponges.
Sea turtles navigate for decades and return to the same beach where they hatched—GPS hearts with flippers.
Sea urchins are spiky pincushions that graze algae with a five-tooth mouth hidden under all those needles.
Seagulls drop clams on rocks to crack them—smart beach chefs!
Male seahorses carry babies in a pouch—dads do the pregnancy!
Sheep can remember faces for years, so a friendly “baa” today might still get recognized long after you’ve grown taller.
Siamese cats are famous talkers, meowing like they have stories to tell, and their bright eyes make every stare feel dramatic.
A siphonophore looks like one animal but is a team of tiny clones linked together—an underwater buddy chain.
Six-gill sharks are ancient deep swimmers with an extra gill slit, like sharks wearing a bonus breathing pocket.
Skunks warn with stomps and tail raises before spraying stinky defense juice!
Sloths move so slowly that algae grows on their fur—walking green gardens!
Snipe eels have beak-like jaws on tiny heads, curving out like deep-sea needles sewing through the dark.
Snow geese fly in huge white Vs that look like rivers in the sky!
Snowy owls hunt in daylight on white wings—Arctic ghosts with yellow eyes!
Sperm whales dive 3,000 ft hunting giant squid—deep-sea monster battles!
Spider monkeys swing by tails like fifth hands—acrobats of the jungle!
Spookfish have mirror eyes that reflect light upward—built-in periscopes!
Squirrels pretend to bury nuts to trick thieves—squirrel spy games!
Starfish can regrow lost arms—some even grow a whole new body from one arm!
Stingrays hide under sand with only eyes peeking out, like shy pancakes waiting to whoosh away.
Stoats do a hopping dance to hypnotize rabbits before a chase!
Stonefish look exactly like rocks—and have the world's most venomous fish spines!
Stoplight loosejaws dangle a glowing red chin lure in the dark, like a secret snack sign only deep-sea fish can read.
Sugar gliders glide through trees like furry kites using skin flaps between their legs!
Tabby cats often sport an M-shaped mark on their forehead, like a tiny tiger crown saying ‘king of the couch.’
A tapir looks like a pig-elephant mix with a mini trunk, perfect for vacuuming up leaves and fruit on the forest floor.
Tarantulas are gentle giants that flick itchy hairs when annoyed!
Tarsiers have eyes bigger than their brain—they see perfectly in the dark!
Tasmanian devils scream and sneeze to talk—it sounds wild but it's just chat!
Tiger sharks wear stripe patterns when young and will try almost any snack—including things that definitely aren’t fish food.
Toucans have huge colorful bills that look heavy but are light as foam!
Triggerfish lock their dorsal spine like a safety switch, then bulldoze through reefs with feisty attitudes.
Tripod fish perch on long fin stilts on the muddy seafloor, standing still like underwater camera tripods waiting for dinner.
Trumpetfish float nose-down and drift like skinny shadows, sneaking up on fish that never see the surprise coming.
Male turkeys puff up and fan their colorful tail feathers to impress friends—and rivals!
Turkey vultures sniff out meals from miles away, doing nature’s cleanup job with wings built for soaring all day.
A turtle carries its “house” on its back and can live for decades, making slow adventures that outlast many people.
Vampire bats share blood meals with hungry roost-mates—real-life food sharing friends!
Vampire squids turn inside out showing spiky cape when scared!
Viperfish flash needle fangs so long they barely fit, lighting up the dark to scare snacks into surprise.
Walruses use giant tusks to pull onto ice and to hug friends in crowded haul-outs!
Weddell seals sing underwater songs that bounce through Antarctic ice, like chilly karaoke stars.
When startled, they flash white tail flags like built-in warning signals!
Wild boar piglets have striped coats like tiny tigers—they blend into forest shadows!
Wolf pups play tag to learn hunting skills—wild puppies at school!
Wolffish look fierce with chunky teeth, but they gently crunch sea urchins like crackers to help the reef.
Wolverines are small but fearless—they chase bears away from food!
Wombats poop cubes so poop doesn't roll away on hills—nature's dice!
Woodpeckers drum on trees 20 times a second without headaches—special shock-proof skulls!
Zombie worms ooze acid to drill into whale bones, then grow roots inside—like tiny gardeners of the deep sea.
